items.correct: Producing a specific quantity of identical items, then switching to make another batch.
items.why: Students confuse batch with mass production. Batch produces FIXED QUANTITIES with the line then changing - between one-off (single item) and mass (continuous high volume).

items.q: Why is injection moulding suited to high-volume production?
items.correct: Tooling cost is high but per-unit cost is very low at scale.
items.misconception: It is the cheapest method for any quantity.
items.random_wrong: It is the only way to make plastic items.
items.why: Students think a 'good' process is good for everything. Injection moulding has expensive moulds - it's only economical when costs are spread over thousands or millions of parts.

items.q: What is the purpose of a jig in manufacturing?
items.correct: To guide a tool and ensure consistent, accurate repeated operations.
items.misconception: To hold the workpiece very tightly so it does not move.
items.why: Students confuse jigs with fixtures. A fixture HOLDS the workpiece; a jig GUIDES the tool (e.g., a drill jig guides the drill bit to the correct position) - though the terms are sometimes used loosely.

items.q: Float glass production is which scale?
items.why: It's Continuous. “Bespoke” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Mass production. “One-off” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If tooling cost is £50,000 and per-unit material cost is £5, total cost for 10,000 units (in £)?
items.why: Exact answer: 100000. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
